category: Error Handling - Propagating Errors Across Threads
prompt: I want to propagate an error from a spawned thread back to the main thread using `?`. I'm using `Box<dyn Error>` as the error type but thread::spawn rejects it because it doesn't implement Send.
error_message:
error[E0277]: `dyn Error` cannot be sent between threads safely
 --> src/main.rs:9:19
  |
9 |     let handle = thread::spawn(|| -> Result<i32, Box<dyn Error>> {
  |                  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^ `dyn Error` cannot be sent between threads safely
  |
  = help: the trait `Send` is not implemented for `dyn Error`
  = note: required because of the requirements on the impl of `Send` for `Box<dyn Error>`
  = note: required because it appears within the type `Result<i32, Box<dyn Error>>`
  = note: required by a bound in `thread::spawn`
explanation: thread::spawn requires the closure to be Send + 'static. The closure's return type is part of what must satisfy Send, and `Box<dyn Error>` is not Send because the underlying error might not be thread-safe. Adding `+ Send + Sync` to the trait object — `Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync>` — constrains the boxed error to only hold Send + Sync types, making the Box itself Send + Sync. A type alias `ThreadError` keeps the signature readable. Note the double `?` at join: the outer unwraps JoinHandle::join's Result<T, Box<dyn Any + Send>> (a panic payload), and the inner propagates the thread's own Result error.

category: Error Handling - Propagating Errors Across Threads
prompt: I'm passing a custom error type to thread::spawn but getting a lifetime error. My error contains a &str message and thread::spawn requires 'static.
error_message:
error[E0597]: `msg` does not live long enough
 --> src/main.rs:18:45
  |
17|     let handle = thread::spawn(move || -> Result<(), ParseError<'_>> {
  |                  - value moved into closure here
18|         Err(ParseError { message: &msg })
  |                                   ^^^^ borrowed value does not live long enough
  |
  = note: requirement occurs because of the type `ParseError<'_>`
  = note: `thread::spawn` requires that the closure is `'static`
explanation: thread::spawn requires the closure to be 'static — it must not borrow anything from the current stack frame because the thread might outlive it. A `ParseError<'a>` holding `&'a str` borrows data tied to a specific lifetime, so it can never be 'static. The fix is to make the error type own its data: `message: String` instead of `message: &str`. The closure then moves `msg` (the owned String) into the ParseError rather than borrowing it. As a rule of thumb, any type you intend to use across thread boundaries should hold owned data or data behind Arc, not raw references.

category: Error Handling - Propagating Errors Across Threads
prompt: I'm spawning multiple threads to process work items and want to collect any errors from them. I tried collecting the JoinHandle results in a loop but I can't figure out how to propagate the first error without also joining remaining threads.
error_message:
error[E0277]: `?` couldn't convert the error to `String`
 --> src/main.rs:18:57
  |
18|         .collect::<Result<Vec<u32>, String>>()?;
  |                                              ^ the trait `From<Box<dyn Any + Send>>` is not implemented for `String`
  |
  = note: the question mark operation (`?`) implicitly converts the error value using the `From` trait
explanation: JoinHandle::join() returns Result<T, Box<dyn Any + Send>> — the outer Err case is a thread panic (not your error type), and the inner T is your Result. Collecting directly into Result<Vec<u32>, String> with ? fails because Box<dyn Any + Send> doesn't convert to String. The fix maps the join error to a string first with map_err, then flattens the nested Result with and_then(|r| r). Collecting a Vec<Result<T,E>> into Result<Vec<T>,E> uses the FromIterator impl that short-circuits on the first Err and collects all Oks into a Vec — a very ergonomic pattern for batch error handling.

category: Error Handling - Propagating Errors Across Threads
prompt: I'm using an mpsc channel to send errors from worker threads back to the main thread, but I accidentally try to send a non-Send error type through the channel and get a compile error.
error_message:
error[E0277]: `Rc<String>` cannot be sent between threads safely
 --> src/main.rs:13:19
  |
13|     thread::spawn(move || {
  |                   ^^^^^^^ `Rc<String>` cannot be sent between threads safely
  |
  = help: within `WorkError`, the trait `Send` is not implemented for `Rc<String>`
  = note: required because it appears within the type `WorkError`
  = note: required because it appears within the type `Result<i32, WorkError>`
  = note: required by a bound in `Sender::<T>::send`
explanation: mpsc::Sender<T> requires T: Send because messages physically cross a thread boundary when sent through a channel. If WorkError holds an Rc<String>, the compiler rejects it because Rc is not Send (its reference count is not atomic). Replacing Rc with Arc (atomic reference count) makes WorkError Send. Alternatively, if the context string is just a static label you can use &'static str or String directly. The general rule: any type you intend to send through a channel or move into a spawned thread must be Send; any type you want to share via Arc must additionally be Sync.

category: Error Handling - Propagating Errors Across Threads
prompt: I'm using anyhow for ergonomic error handling and want to use it inside thread::spawn. The anyhow::Error type appears to work but I want to confirm the pattern and understand why it works when Box<dyn Error> doesn't.
error_message:
error[E0277]: `dyn Error` cannot be sent between threads safely
 --> src/main.rs:13:19
  |
13|     let handle = thread::spawn(|| risky_work(5));
  |                  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^ `dyn Error` cannot be sent between threads safely
  |
  = help: the trait `Send` is not implemented for `dyn Error`
  = note: required because of the requirements on the impl of `Send` for `Box<dyn Error>`
  = note: required by a bound in `thread::spawn`
explanation: anyhow::Error is internally `Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync + 'static>`, so it is Send + Sync by construction. This is why anyhow works seamlessly with thread::spawn while plain `Box<dyn Error>` does not. The `anyhow::Result<T>` alias (`type Result<T, E = Error> = std::result::Result<T, E>`) makes function signatures concise. anyhow is the recommended choice for application-level code where you want ergonomic error propagation without defining custom error types; thiserror is preferred for library code where callers need to match on error variants.
